content.problemStatement:
Ryan is a boy from a small town,  who has been given a task by his father. He has NNN wooden planks, numbered from 111 to NNN, which are colored either black or white.

His task is to color all planks the same color! But there is some magic in the winds of his small town. Whenever he colors the ithi^{th}ith ( plank which has the color SiS_iSi​ ) to a color PPP then following events happen:


if 2≤i≤N2 \leq i \leq N2≤i≤N and Si=Si−1S_i = S_{i - 1}Si​=Si−1​, then color of (i−1)th(i - 1)^{th}(i−1)th plank changes to PPP.
if 1≤i≤N−11 \leq i \leq N - 11≤i≤N−1 and Si=Si+1S_i = S_{i + 1}Si​=Si+1​, then color of (i+1)th(i + 1)^{th}(i+1)th plank changes to PPP.
Now this process continues for the newly colored planks also. If none of the neighbors have same color, then nothing happens to the neighbors.

Suppose Ryan has planks which have their coloring : BBWWWBB B W W W BBBWWWB
If Ryan colors the fourth plank( whose color is WWW ) to color BBB, then the finally the planks would be colored as following:

BBBBBBB B B B B BBBBBBB

Ryan can choose any **one** of the NNN planks and change its color as many times as he wants. Determine the minimum number of times Ryan has to paint a plank such that all planks get the same color at the end.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
The first line of each test case consists of an integer NNN the number of planks
Second line of each test case consists of a string SSS of size NNN,where the iii th character denotes the color of plank iii


Output Format
For each testcase, output a single integer denoting the minimum number of times Ryan has to paint a **single** plank such that all planks get the same color at the end.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 100000
S consists only of characters 'B' and 'W'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the number of planks. The second line contains a string S of length N consisting only of characters 'B' and 'W', where the i‑th character denotes the colour of the i‑th plank.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the minimum number of times Ryan has to paint the chosen plank so that all planks become the same colour.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we count the number of adjacent unequal pairs (cnt) and output ceil(cnt/2). The counts are: 2, 3, 0, 2 respectively, giving answers 1, 2, 0, 1.

solution.approach: When Ryan paints a plank, the whole maximal monochromatic segment (run) containing that plank changes to the new colour. After the change the segment may merge with the neighbouring runs if they have the same colour. Therefore one painting operation can eliminate at most two colour‑change borders – one on the left side and one on the right side – and it eliminates two borders exactly when the colours of the left and right neighbouring runs are equal. Let cnt be the number of positions i (1 ≤ i < N) where S[i] ≠ S[i+1]; cnt is the total number of colour‑change borders in the whole string. Each operation can remove at most two borders, so the minimum number of operations is ceil(cnt / 2). This value is always achievable by repeatedly recolouring the current segment to the colour of the farther side, merging two equal coloured neighbours whenever possible. The algorithm therefore reduces to counting the number of adjacent unequal pairs and outputting (cnt + 1) // 2.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case – a single linear scan to count colour changes.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        s = next(it)
        cnt = sum(1 for i in range(n-1) if s[i] != s[i+1])
        out.append(str((cnt + 1)//2))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
